meta.title: BMW 216d Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 216d engine — B37 1.5 3-cylinder diesel. Active Tourer, Gran Tourer and Gran Coupé, real UK costs and reliability. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 216d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned B37 engine typically costs £2,200–£3,800 supply only, with the same engine used across the 2 Series Active Tourer, Gran Tourer, Gran Coupé and X1 16d. Fitting adds £900–£1,400. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

The B37 is rated ★★★★☆ — significantly more reliable than the N47 it replaced. The timing chain is at the front of the engine, eliminating the catastrophic rear-chain failure mode. However, DPF clogging, EGR cooler issues, and occasional timing chain tensioner wear have been reported. Regular motorway driving is the key to longevity.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 216d?

acceptedAnswer.text: DPF clogging (urban use), EGR cooler coolant leaks, and occasional timing chain tensioner wear are the most common issues. None typically lead to full engine replacement if caught early — regular DPF maintenance is the priority.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 216d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a £5,000–£12,000 216d, a £3,000 recon engine is a manageable 25–60% of the vehicle's value — worth it for a well-maintained example. Given the 2 Series' strong retained values, replacement is usually viable for vehicles in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 216d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 216d uses the B37 1.5-litre 3-cylinder diesel across all body styles. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the B37's DPF, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — DPF cleaning or replacement is a straightforward repair costing £400–£1,200. The B37's DPF is the most common service item on this engine; regular motorway runs are the best prevention. If caught early, it does not lead to engine replacement.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 216d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full BMW service history with documented oil-change intervals
* Evidence of regular motorway driving (to clear the DPF)
* Post-2020 build with any early EGR software updates applied
* Good MOT history with no repeated emissions failures
avoidIf: * Service history shows only urban driving patterns (DPF risk)
* Engine warning light present with no diagnosis documentation
* Repeated coolant top-ups with no diagnosis
* Any evidence of DPF warning lights or regeneration failures

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 216d's B37 is a modern, efficient diesel — significantly more reliable than the N47 it replaced. The front-mounted timing chain and improved architecture make this a safer bet, but DPF and EGR issues remain the watchpoints. Buy on service history and proof of regular use, not just low mileage.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 216d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle on startup (timing chain tensioner wear — far less common than N47)
* Service History — Including which engine era (B37 is the only 216d engine — verify this before purchase)
* DPF Regeneration — Has the vehicle had regular motorway runs? Short journeys accelerate DPF wear
* Smoke Check — White smoke on startup can indicate EGR coolant leak
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 5,000 miles? If so, investigate
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved emissions or EGR issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures; repeated DPF advisories are a red flag
* Turbo Boost — Test acceleration; any hesitation, lag, or whistling?
